topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the dull thud of something heavy dropping onto a wooden desk. My fingers hovered over the screen, the keyboard feeling strangely unresponsive beneath my touch. It was a notification about an article, one I had poured weeks of quiet, focused energy into crafting. Then the text hit me: the byline was wrong. It belonged to someone else. A name that felt utterly foreign, a stranger wearing my intellectual skin. A slow, cold ache settled in my chest, heavy and suffocating. I stared at the screen, the fluorescent light above my head buzzing with irritating persistence. I didn't move for a long time, just tracing the outline of the offending text with my gaze, a strange numbness spreading through my limbs.

[story 2]

I picked up the physical copy of the publication. The glossy paper felt slick and alien in my hands. Every word swam before my eyes, but the discrepancy was glaring, undeniable. The author listed was not mine. A profound emptiness washed over me, a kind of internal vacuum where excitement used to reside. I set the paper down on the floor, the sound barely registering. My shoulders slumped forward, a deliberate collapse of posture. I walked to the window, watching the traffic flow below, the movement seeming distant and irrelevant. My breath came in shallow, even puffs, and the world outside felt muted, as if viewed through thick, dusty glass.

[story 3]

The notification pinged, and I blinked slowly, the movement requiring conscious effort. When I opened the file, the realization struck not with a jolt, but with a slow, pervasive sinking feeling. The attribution was flawed. Someone else had claimed my words. A strange stillness descended, a quiet withdrawal of all internal momentum. I leaned back in my chair, the worn leather groaning softly under my weight. My gaze drifted toward the wall, focusing on a small imperfection in the paint. There was no immediate urge to correct it, no sudden burst of anger, just a heavy inertia, a profound lack of desire to engage with the task at hand.
